nodes.title: AlexNet
nodes.description: Landmark machine learning breakthrough

nodes.title: Quantum annealing
nodes.subtitle: D-Wave One
nodes.description: Controversial whether this is actually a practical technology

nodes.title: LTE and 4G cellular network
nodes.description: The definition of 4G was retroactively amended to include LTE, making 2009 the date of its first commercialization

nodes.title: SMS
nodes.subtitle: aka Short message service
nodes.description: International development: GSM group in Germany and France, then first implementation in the UK by Vodafone, and first commercial deployment in Sweden

nodes.title: Antivirus software
nodes.description: From Wikipedia: "There are competing claims for the innovator of the first antivirus product. Possibly, the first publicly documented removal of an "in the wild" computer virus (the "Vienna virus") was performed by Bernd Fix in 1987. In 1987, Andreas Lüning and Kai Figge, who founded G Data Software in 1985, released their first antivirus product for the Atari ST platform. In 1987, the Ultimate Virus Killer (UVK) was also released. This was the de facto industry standard virus killer for the Atari ST and Atari Falcon, the last version of which (version 9.0) was released in April 2004. In 1987, in the United States, John McAfee founded the McAfee company and, at the end of that year, he released the first version of VirusScan. Also in 1987 (in Czechoslovakia), Peter Paško, Rudolf Hrubý, and Miroslav Trnka created the first version of NOD antivirus."

nodes.title: Lithium-ion battery
nodes.description: modern Li-ion battery. There were earlier ones (see lithium battery pacemakers in the 1970s)

nodes.title: Active-pixel sensor
nodes.subtitle: aka CMOS sensor
nodes.description: Active-pixel and CMOS sensors aren't exactly the same thing, but CMOS is by far the most common type of APS today

nodes.title: Home computer
nodes.subtitle: The 1977 trinity: Commodore PET, Apple II, and TRS-80

nodes.title: DNA sequencing
nodes.description: Sanger sequencing and Maxam-Gilbert sequencing were developed independently approximately in the same year (1977). They use different techniques. Sanger sequencing became more widespread and is the basis for further sequencing technology.

nodes.title: EEPROM
nodes.subtitle: aka Electrically erasable programmable read-only memory

nodes.title: Microcomputer
nodes.subtitle: Micral
nodes.description: Fist commercially available microprocessor-based computer: the Micral

nodes.title: Programmable calculator
nodes.subtitle: Mathatronics Mathatron
nodes.description: The Programma 101 by Olivetti is often considered the first programmable calculator, although it was preceded by the much more obscure Mathatron.

nodes.title: Messenger RNA
nodes.subtitle: aka mRNA
nodes.description: Discovery credit is usually considered shared among several people

nodes.title: Intercontinental ballistic missile
nodes.subtitle: R-7 Semyorka
nodes.description: R-7 Semyorka was both a military missile and a rocket that led to the development of the Vostok rockets, which launched Sputnik and the Vostok capsule into space

nodes.title: Float glass
nodes.description: Inventor and company have the same name by coincidence

nodes.title: Video game
nodes.subtitle: Bertie the Brain
nodes.description: arguably the first video game

nodes.title: Zippe-type centrifuge
nodes.description: Technique for uranium enrichment

nodes.title: Stored-program computer
nodes.subtitle: Manchester Baby
nodes.description: Von Neumann architecture sometimes considered a synonym, though that is sometimes criticized (technically, the Harvard architecture also applies to some early stored-program computers)

nodes.title: Synchrotron
nodes.description: independently invented by Veksler in 1944 and MacMillan in 1945. First one built in 1945

nodes.title: Electronic general purpose computer
nodes.subtitle: ENIAC
nodes.description: ENIAC: first programmable, electronic, general-purpose digital computer

nodes.title: DNA as the carrier of information
nodes.subtitle: Avery–MacLeod–McCarty experiment

nodes.title: Calutron
nodes.description: Technique for uranium enrichment

nodes.title: Epoxy resin
nodes.description: Simultaneous and independent developments by Schlack and Castan in Germany and Switzerland

nodes.title: Gaseous diffusion
nodes.description: Technique for uranium enrichment

nodes.title: Kroll process
nodes.description: The Kroll process made pure titanium economical, leading to many applications starting in the 1950s.

nodes.title: Electromechanical programmable computer
nodes.subtitle: Z1
nodes.description: The Z1: first freely programmable computer in the world

nodes.title: Helicopter
nodes.subtitle: Focke-Wulf Fw 61
nodes.description: the Focke-Wulf Fw 61 was the first practical helicopter

nodes.title: Convulsive therapy
nodes.description: Uses the drug metrazol to induce seizure; replaced by electroconvulsive therapy after 1938

nodes.title: Acrylic glass
nodes.subtitle: aka Poly(methyl methacrylate), PMMA, Plexiglas, Lucite

nodes.title: Aircraft carrier with full-length flight deck
nodes.subtitle: HMS Argus

nodes.title: Superheterodyne radio receiver
nodes.description: Design of virtually all modern radio receivers

nodes.title: Fire-control system
nodes.subtitle: Dreyer fire control table and Argo clock

nodes.title: Cellulose acetate film
nodes.subtitle: aka Safety film
nodes.description: aka "safety film" because it's less flammable than nitrocellulose

nodes.title: Chromosome theory of inheritance
nodes.description: independent work by Boveri and Sutton around the same time

nodes.title: Singing arc
nodes.description: Arguably one of the first instances of electronic music

nodes.title: Oscilloscope
nodes.description: perfected in 1899 by Jonathan Zenneck

nodes.title: Acheson process
nodes.description: Process to make synthetic graphite and silicon carbide

nodes.title: Hampson–Linde air liquefaction
nodes.description: gas liquefaction / air separation. Independently invented in the same year by Linde and Hampson

nodes.title: Lumière cinematograph
nodes.description: the Lumière brothers bought the rights to the name and patent and then applied them to what was mostly their own invention

nodes.title: Helium (isolation)
nodes.description: independently isolated in the UK and in Sweden during the same year

nodes.title: Calcium carbide production
nodes.description: independently discovered by Willson and Moissan in the same year

nodes.title: Movie camera
nodes.subtitle: Kinetograph
nodes.description: The Kinetograph is arguably the first true movie camera

nodes.title: Safety bicycle
nodes.description: design of modern bicycles

nodes.title: Automatic machine gun
nodes.subtitle: Maxim gun
nodes.description: The Maxim gun: first practical self-powered machine gun

nodes.title: Electric tram
nodes.description: The picture shows the Gross-Lichterfelde tram in Berlin in 1882, the first commercially successful line

nodes.title: Light bulb
nodes.description: This date corresponds to commercially successful light bulbs, but there were many earlier attempts. Independently invented by Swan and Edison

nodes.title: Liquid oxygen
nodes.description: Independently obtained by Louis Paul Cailletet in France and Raoul Pictet in Switzerland. Zygmunt Wróblewski and Karol Olszewski made more significant amounts in 1883 (see liquid nitrogen entry)

nodes.title: Carbon microphone
nodes.description: Considered to have been independently invented by Hughes in England and Edison and Berliner in the US

nodes.title: Liquefied gas refrigerants
nodes.description: Linde's "new process made possible using gases such as ammonia, sulfur dioxide (SO2) and methyl chloride (CH3Cl) as refrigerants and they were widely used for that purpose until the late 1920s."

nodes.title: Maxwell's equations
nodes.description: work done over many years, especially at Glenlair House in Scotland, and published in full form in 1873 when Maxwell was professor at Cambridge

nodes.title: Typewriter with QWERTY keyboard
nodes.subtitle: Sholes and Glidden typewriter / Remington No. 1

nodes.title: Corrugated fiberboard
nodes.subtitle: aka Corrugated cardboard
nodes.description: Improved by Oliver Long in 1874 by having liner sheets on both sides of the corrugated part

nodes.title: Celluloid
nodes.description: improvement on Parkesine, using camphor

nodes.title: Helium (discovery)
nodes.description: Lockyer and Janssen are generally jointly credited with the discovery, though it can be argued that Janssen doesn't deserve the credit.

nodes.title: Steam velocipede
nodes.description: Both the Michaux-Perreaux and the Roper velocipedes have competing claims at being the first steam-powered velocipede

nodes.title: Siemens–Martin process
nodes.description: named after Carl Wilhelm Siemens who  invented the open hearth furnace, which Martin then used to make steel

nodes.title: Mendelian inheritance
nodes.subtitle: aka Mendelian genetics
nodes.description: forgotten then rediscovered in 1900

nodes.title: Color photography
nodes.description: idea by Maxwell in 1855, first photo in 1861 by Sutton

nodes.title: Bessemer process
nodes.description: The modern process is named after its inventor, the Englishman Henry Bessemer, who took out a patent on the process in 1856. The process was said to be independently discovered in 1851 by the American inventor William Kelly though the claim is controversial

nodes.title: Thermionic emission
nodes.subtitle: aka Edison effect
nodes.description: re-discovered by Frederick Guthrie in 1873 and Thomas Edison in 1880. The Edison discovery led to its use in vacuum tubes.

nodes.title: Binocular microscope
nodes.description: In the 1850s, John Leonard Riddell, Professor of Chemistry at Tulane University, invented the first practical binocular microscope while carrying out one of the earliest and most extensive American microscopic investigations of cholera.

nodes.title: Yale pin-tumbler lock
nodes.description: modern lock design

nodes.title: Modern Portland cement
nodes.description: Significantly distinct from the original Portland cement of 1824, developed by William Aspdin's father Joseph. Priority disputed between Willam Aspdin and Isaac Charles Johnson.

nodes.title: Recording telegraph
nodes.subtitle: aka Bain's facsimile
nodes.description: Further improvements by Frederick Bakewell. Earliest version of a fax machine.

nodes.title: Steam hammer
nodes.description: independently invented in 1839 by Nasmyth in Scotland and Bourdon in France

nodes.title: Induction coil
nodes.subtitle: aka Inductorium, Ruhmkorff coil
nodes.description: Independently invented by Callan and Page

nodes.title: Vapor-compression refrigeration system
nodes.description: John Gorrie made a similar attempt a few years latter, in 1842

nodes.title: Phenakistiscope
nodes.description: Can also be considered the invention of the stroboscope

nodes.title: Electromagnetic induction
nodes.description: independently discovered by Michael Faraday in 1831 and Joseph Henry in 1832

nodes.title: Photography
nodes.subtitle: View from the Window at Le Gras

nodes.title: Döbereiner's lamp
nodes.description: one of the first lighters

nodes.title: Pattern-tracing lathe
nodes.description: apparently independent and different from Nartov's copying lathe?

nodes.title: Wire wheel
nodes.description: Eugène Meyer got a patent in 1868, but the invention goes back to 1808

nodes.title: Fourdrinier machine
nodes.description: Improvement of the paper machine by Robert

nodes.title: Sodium bicarbonate
nodes.subtitle: aka Baking soda, Bicarbonate of soda

nodes.title: Submarine
nodes.subtitle: Nautilus
nodes.description: the Nautilus, the first practical submarine

nodes.title: Electric arc
nodes.description: independently by Davy (1800, short) and Petrov (1802, long continuous arcs)

nodes.title: Glider
nodes.description: Scientific principles for heavier-than-air, man-carrying gliders were first published in 1799 by Cayley

nodes.title: Roman cement
nodes.description: unrelated to Roman concrete

nodes.title: Airship with manual propulsion
nodes.description: crossed the English channel in 1785

nodes.title: Meusnier's dirigible
nodes.description: designed used by the Robert brothers and Jacques Charles in 1784

nodes.title: Hygrometer
nodes.subtitle: Hair tension hygrometer
nodes.description: There was controversy with Deluc, also of Geneva, who invented a whalebone hygrometer perhaps a few years before.

nodes.title: Oxygen (Scheele–Priestley)
nodes.description: independently (re)discovered by Scheele and Priestley. Scheele discovered it earlier but Priestley often gets credit due to having published first

nodes.title: Nickel silver
nodes.subtitle: aka German silver, Paktong, Maillechort
nodes.description: Alloy of copper, nickel, and zinc; does not contain silver!

nodes.title: Lever escapement
nodes.description: Escapement used in almost all modern mechanical watches

nodes.title: Balance spring
nodes.description: Disputed priority between Hooke and Huygens

nodes.title: Magdeburg hemispheres
nodes.description: Experiment used to demonstrate the power of atmospheric pressure.

nodes.title: Oxygen (Sendigovius)
nodes.description: very likely discovered by Sendigovius in 1604, but then re-discovered in the 1770s, from which modern oxygen chemistry derives

nodes.title: Xun lei chong
nodes.description: a revolving-barrel, spear-combined musket

nodes.title: Revolver
nodes.description: There were earlier designs of revolving firearms, e.g. c. 1580, but Stopler's design is often called the first revolver

nodes.title: Sector
nodes.subtitle: aka Sector rule, Proportional compass, Military compass

nodes.title: Hydrochloric acid
nodes.subtitle: aka Muriatic acid, Spirits of salt

nodes.title: Laudanum
nodes.description: Paracelsus popularized opium in Europe and coined the word laudanum, but his opium pill differed significantly from Sydenham's tincture of opium in the 1660s, which became standard until the 20th century

nodes.title: Naval mine
nodes.description: Naval mines were first invented by Chinese innovators of Imperial China and were described in thorough detail by the early Ming dynasty artillery officer Jiao Yu, in his 14th-century military treatise known as the Huolongjing.

nodes.title: Multistage rocket
nodes.subtitle: Huolongchushui
nodes.description: An illustration and description in the 14th century Chinese Huolongjing by Jiao Yu and Liu Bowen shows the oldest known multistage rocket; this was the "fire-dragon issuing from the water" (火龙出水, huǒ lóng chū shuǐ), which was used mostly by the Chinese navy.

nodes.title: Worm drive roller gin
nodes.description: 13th or 14th century: Worm gear cotton gin in Peninsular India (i.e. probably under the Yadava dynasty although may also be the Vijayanagara Empire or Bahmani Sultanate).

nodes.title: Cannon
nodes.subtitle: Wuwei Bronze Cannon
nodes.description: "The earliest known depiction of a cannon is a sculpture from the Dazu Rock Carvings in Sichuan dated to 1128, however, the earliest archaeological samples and textual accounts do not appear until the 13th century. The primary extant specimens of cannon from the 13th century are the Wuwei Bronze Cannon dated to 1227, the Heilongjiang hand cannon dated to 1288, and the Xanadu Gun dated to 1298. However, only the Xanadu gun contains an inscription bearing a date of production, so it is considered the earliest confirmed extant cannon." The image shows the Heilongjiang hand cannon.

nodes.title: Junk rig
nodes.subtitle: Chinese lugsail
nodes.description: Not Chinese in origin, although typically associated with Chinese junks

nodes.title: Eruptor
nodes.description: Intermediate form between fire lances and cannons, when fire lances began being made of metal and put on frames

nodes.title: Steelmaking with partial decarbonization
nodes.description: Medieval process akin to the modern Bessemer process

nodes.title: Paper money
nodes.subtitle: Jiaozi
nodes.description: Jiaozi: considered the first true paper money

nodes.title: Castle
nodes.subtitle: Château de Doué-la-Fontaine
nodes.description: image shows the Castle Site of Montbazon, built in the late 10th or 11th century

nodes.title: Fierce-fire oil cabinet
nodes.subtitle: aka Pen Huo Qi
nodes.description: Double-piston pump naphtha flamethrower; Chinese version of Greek fire, plausibly acquired indirectly (through Arabia) from the West

nodes.title: Flying cash
nodes.description: Precursor to true banknotes during Tang dynasty

nodes.title: Positional decimal numerals and true zero
nodes.subtitle: Hindu-Arabic numerals

nodes.title: Pointed arch bridge
nodes.subtitle: Karamagara Bridge
nodes.description: Image shows the Getar bridge in Armenia

nodes.title: Dichroic glass
nodes.subtitle: Lycurgus cup
nodes.description: data for the Lycurgus cup, the earliest known item made of dichroic glass

nodes.title: Onager
nodes.description: This is the design most commonly associated with catapults, which was a generic word for artillery in Greco-Roman times

nodes.title: Papermaking
nodes.description: Cai Lun is generally credited as the inventor of paper, but there is archeological evidence that paper predates him by centuries. He likely came up with method improvements that turned paper into an industry.

nodes.title: Pivoted scissors
nodes.description: Modern scissor design

nodes.title: Soap
nodes.description: Attributed to "the Gauls" by Pliny

nodes.title: Trip hammer
nodes.description: "Although Chinese historians assert that its origins may span as far back as the Zhou Dynasty (1050 BC–221 BC), the British sinologist Joseph Needham regards the earliest texts to describe the device are the Jijiupian dictionary of 40 BC" and other texts from 15 BC and 20 AD

nodes.title: Segmental arched bridge
nodes.subtitle: Ponte San Lorenzo
nodes.description: Image shows the Ponte Molino, another Roman-era segmental arch bridge in Padua

nodes.title: Analog computer
nodes.subtitle: Antikythera mechanism
nodes.description: Antikythera mechanism possibly from Rhodes, possibly involved Hipparchus.

nodes.title: Water organ
nodes.subtitle: aka Hydraulis
nodes.description: Ctesibius' most commonly known invention today is a pipe organ (hydraulis), a predecessor of the modern church organ.

nodes.title: Gimbal
nodes.description: Philo described it and the invention is sometimes attributed to him

nodes.title: Animal-driven rotary mill
nodes.description: possibly in Carthaginian Sardinia

nodes.title: Crossbow (Greece)
nodes.subtitle: Gastraphetes
nodes.description: It's possible that crossbows spread from China to Europe, but there are differences in mechanism

nodes.title: Rotary quern
nodes.description: possibly in Celtiberian Spain, possibly in Carthage

nodes.title: Cataract surgery
nodes.subtitle: Couching
nodes.description: speculatively, may be mentioned in Mesopotamian and Egyptian sources

nodes.title: Wagonway
nodes.subtitle: Diolkos
nodes.description: the Diolkos, built to move ships across the isthmus of Corinth

nodes.title: Magnifying glass
nodes.subtitle: Nimrud lens
nodes.description: speculative: the Nimrud lens may have been a magnifying glass, or just a decorative item. There are clear references to magnifying glasses in Aristophanes (5th century BC)

nodes.title: Writing (Mesoamerica)
nodes.subtitle: Olmec script
nodes.description: Controversial

nodes.title: Smalt
nodes.subtitle: aka Cobalt glass
nodes.description: Glass colored with cobalt oxide is called "smalt" when ground into a pigment

nodes.title: Water clock
nodes.subtitle: aka Clepsydra
nodes.description: May need to split this entry into various regional water clocks. This entry currently reflects Egyptian clocks.

nodes.title: Copper pipes
nodes.description: Pyramid of Sahure in Egypt

nodes.title: Scroll
nodes.description: The oldest known scroll is the Diary of Merer, which can be dated to c. 2568 BCE.

nodes.title: Reservoir
nodes.description: Image of the Great Hafir in Sudan

nodes.title: Sewn boat building
nodes.subtitle: Abydos boats
nodes.description: Image shows the Khufu ship, one of the earliest sewn boats (2500 BCE)

nodes.title: Plumbing
nodes.description: clay pipes in the Temple of Bel at Nippur

nodes.title: Fast potter's wheel
nodes.description: various possible origins: Sumer, southeast Europe, China, Indus Valley civilizaton

nodes.title: Wheel
nodes.description: It is not known whether Chinese, Indians, Europeans and even Sumerians invented the wheel independently or not.

nodes.title: Egyptian faience
nodes.description: Unrelated to the other material called "faience"

nodes.title: Tournette
nodes.subtitle: aka Slow potter's wheel
nodes.description: aka slow wheel, a precursor to the fast potter's wheel

nodes.title: Boomerang
nodes.subtitle: Returning Throwing Stick
nodes.description: Specifically returning boomerangs. Also independently invented elsewhere (North America, Egypt, Europe)
